---
title: "Introduction"
---

::: section-badge
Phase I · The Journalist
:::

The cozy game genre has emerged as one of the defining issues in contemporary public discourse. How media organizations frame this topic shapes public understanding, political opinion, and social behavior, all starting with the rise of the genre during the COVID-19 pandemic in 2020. Despite its importance, relatively little systematic research has examined how Judith Butler's Gender Performativity Theory, using YouTube gameplay "first time" videos, specifically covers the cozy game genre — a gap this study addresses.

## Background and Significance

Prior research has consistently demonstrated that media coverage plays a powerful role in shaping audience perceptions of the video gaming community, specifically around the genre of cozy games. While cozy games have been around for decades with notable titles such as *The Sims* (Electronic Arts, 2000) and *Story of Seasons* (Marvelous Inc., 2014), previously known as *Harvest Moon* (Amccus, 1996), they rose in popularity during the COVID-19 pandemic. According to @boudreauWhoseVibeIt2025, her article *Whose Vibe is it Anyway?* involves what games count as "cozy games," and mentions how "the selected range of publication dates from 2019-2024, which coincides with the rise in popularity of cozy games specifically through and beyond the COVID-19 pandemic." Studies examining the rise of cozy games have found that it's biggest motivator, besides COVID-19 though everyone transitioning to being online was a major factor, was how women and minorities were treated by men when playing in online communities. @coteCasualResistanceLongitudinal2020 talks about in her article studying gender construction of video games that those who were not men "faced higher levels of harassment in online gaming communities," leading to a later debate on whether or not casual games were considered "true" video games at all. @tangMensHarassmentBehavior2016 showed that, when it came to harassment in online gaming communities, "men are most likely to be the perpetrator's, and those perceived to be as minorities are often targeted as they do not fit within the dominant social identity." Despite all of this, there is still relatively little studies on how @butler1988 Gender Performativity Theory plays a part in a genre targeted towards women and minorities. Most existing studies focus on the culture that shifts around it, or how men respond to others in online communities, and not necessarily how they perform their gender in a genre intending to be soft and easier to manage. This leaves the cozy game genre and any connections to gender performance relatively understudied.

YouTube Gameplay videos, specifically around a player's first experience with recognizable cozy game *Stardew Valley* (Concerned Ape, 2016), and newer title inspired by Concerned Ape's game, *Fields of Mistria* (NPC Studio, 2024) represents a particularly important site of analysis because of the popularity of gameplay videos, as well as a player's first reactions to either game. There's a popularity in a player's first-time experience with a cozy farming game, along with a player's "100 Days" videos of either title. By focusing on these videos with a game that's knowingly targeted for women and minorities, it allows the opportunity for gender performance to still be acted out along with which gaming mechanics different genders are drawn to, if any.

## Theoretical Framework

This study is grounded in Judith Butler's Gender Performativity Theory proposes that gender identity is more of a performance through the actor's repeated actions through the eyes of their society and, if done well or not, receives praise or punishment by those who also perform by their gender @butler1988 . A defining feature of this framework is gender being a *repeated* performance, shown and set by the culture and society that individual is raised in. It means that the way a person acts based on their gender identity is because society has taught them, overtime and through repeated reactions, that's how they're supposed to act. If they go against their gender performance, or it's challenged in a specific way, society and those actors in that set of performances will shun or punish them in some way. If they act as part of their usual gender performance, they'll be praised and encouraged by those same individuals.

Applied to the present study, Gender Performativity Theory suggests that a player's first experience in either cozy game title would reflect some gender performances to fill the gap. This could also be influenced on whether or not they were experiencing it for the first time on a livestream or through multiple YouTube gameplay videos. The baseline of their gender performance would be reflected in either way. This makes it an appropriate framework for analyzing gender, gameplay videos, gaming mechanics, gender performance, farm choice, and seasons because it takes into consideration the acting out of performance based on how far along in eiter gaming title the player was in. Gender and farm choice would've been something chosen at the beginning during the character creation phase, then the performance of that gender would show how they respond during gameplay videos, what gaming mechanic's they're drawn to, and how far along they made it in their gameplay videos based on what season they're in.

The article examined in the Annotated Manuscript — @boudreauWhoseVibeIt2025 — provides a direct application of this framework to gender performance with the cozy game genre. Boudreau uses Social Construction Theory as a way to explain how cozy games are as a genre, as well as how our society constructs what it means to be a "cozy game." It mentions how it's subjective and "personal" per player and by person @boudreauWhoseVibeIt2025 , making it a unique way to look at @butler1988 Gender Performativity Theory. If the genre itself is already subjective, then it would make the idea of their performance the baseline going into said subjective gaming genre.

## Research Questions

Based on the gaps in the existing literature and the predictive logic of gender performativity, this study addresses the following research questions:

> **RQ1:** How would YouTube gameplay videos frame how different genders play cozy games?

> **RQ2:** What is the relationship between gender performance and how the interact with their first "100 Days in *Stardew Valley* or *Fields of Mistria*" YouTube gameplay videos?

Together, these questions aim to produce a systematic, empirically grounded account of how YouTube gameplay videos of a player's first experiences covers cozy games and gender performance — one that moves beyond anecdotal observation toward replicable measurement.
